title: Formal vs Informal Language
description: Adapt your language to different contexts — from casual texts to professional emails and formal writing.

items.back: Formal: I am writing. / Informal: I'm writing.
items.example: Dear Mr Johnson, I am writing to… (NOT: I'm writing to…)

items.back: find out → discover / go up → increase / put off → postpone
items.example: The deadline has been postponed. (formal) / We've put the deadline off. (informal)

items.back: Dear Sir/Madam → Yours faithfully / Dear Ms Lee → Yours sincerely / Hi → Take care / Cheers
items.example: Dear Mr Smith, … Yours sincerely, Anna. / Hey Tom, … Cheers, Anna.
The project is on schedule. Furthermore, we are under budget.
The project is on schedule. Also, we're under budget!

items.back: I would suggest… / It may be advisable… / One could argue…
items.example: I would suggest reviewing the contract before signing. (NOT: You should check it)

items.back: get → obtain / tell → inform / think → consider / need → require
items.example: We require further information. (formal) / We need more info. (informal)

items.front: Passive voice is more common in formal writing
items.back: Active: We have reviewed your application. / Passive: Your application has been reviewed.
items.example: The report was submitted on time. (formal report) / We sent the report on time. (informal)

items.prompt: You are writing a formal email to a client. Which greeting is most appropriate?
items.explanation: 'Dear Ms Thompson,' is the correct formal salutation for a business email.

items.prompt: Which sentence is more appropriate in a formal report?
options: We found out the product has a fault.
It was discovered that the product contains a defect.
items.explanation: Passive voice, formal vocabulary (discovered, defect, contains) → formal. 'Found out' and 'got a problem' are informal.

items.prompt: What is the formal equivalent of 'We need to put off the meeting'?
options: We need to delay the meeting
items.explanation: Passive + Latin verb (postponed) makes this the most formal option.

items.prompt: Which linking word is most appropriate in a formal essay?
items.explanation: 'Furthermore' is a formal connector. 'Also', 'plus', and 'and also' are informal/neutral.

items.prompt: Which sign-off is correct when you know the name of the recipient?
items.explanation: 'Yours sincerely' when you know the name. 'Yours faithfully' when you DON'T know the name (Dear Sir/Madam).

items.sentence: Informal: 'We need more info.' → Formal: 'We ___ further information.'
Informal: 'We found out there's a problem.' → Formal: 'It was ___ that an issue exists.'
Informal: 'We put off the deadline.' → Formal: 'The deadline has been ___.'
Formal email sign-off when you know the person's name: 'Yours ___,'
Informal: 'Also, we're under budget.' → Formal: '___, we are within budget.'
